rows.title: China TC260 Issues Basic Requirements for Security of Generative AI Services
rows.summary:
China's National Information Security Standardisation Technical Committee (TC260) has published a national standard guideline, designated TC260-003, setting out basic security requirements for generative artificial intelligence services, applicable across the People's Republic of China.

On 11 February 2024, TC260 released the document titled «Basic Security Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services» (生成式人工智能服务安全基本要求), establishing a technical framework for assessing and managing security risks associated with generative AI systems made available to the public within China.

The guidelines specify requirements across several categories. First, they address training data security, requiring that data used to train generative AI models be screened for content that could undermine national security, incite discrimination, or contain personal information collected without lawful basis. The guidelines set quantitative thresholds for data quality, including a requirement that harmful content in training corpora not exceed specified proportions. Second, the guidelines address model security, covering resistance to adversarial inputs, model output controllability, and the obligation to conduct security assessments before a generative AI service is made available. Third, the guidelines establish requirements for generated content security, specifying that outputs involving specific sensitive topic categories, including political content, ethnicity, religion, and violence, be subject to filtering and blocking controls. The document also sets requirements for the labelling of AI-generated content, consistent with the Measures for the Management of Deep Synthesis Internet Information Services issued by the Cyberspace Administration of China.

The guidelines further require that service providers maintain mechanisms for user complaint and reporting, retain logs of generated content and user interactions for a period specified in the document, and conduct periodic security self-assessments. The TC260-003 guidelines function as a recommended national standard (推荐性标准) and provide the technical baseline referenced by regulators, including the Cyberspace Administration of China, when evaluating compliance with the Interim Measures for the Management of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services that entered into force on 15 August 2023.

rows.title: China Cyberspace Administration Publishes Draft Interim Measures for Anthropomorphic AI Interaction Services for Public Consultation
rows.summary:
China's Cyberspace Administration has released draft interim measures governing anthropomorphic artificial intelligence interaction services for public consultation, setting out content restrictions and disclosure obligations for service providers operating in mainland China.

On 27 December 2025, the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) published the Draft Interim Measures for the Administration of Anthropomorphic Interaction AI Services, inviting public feedback on the proposed regulatory framework.

The draft measures establish a series of prohibitions applicable to both providers and users of anthropomorphic AI interaction services. The CAC stipulated that such parties are prohibited from generating or disseminating content that endangers national security, undermines national honour and interests, disrupts ethnic unity, facilitates illegal religious activities, or incites criminal conduct. The draft further prohibits the spread of rumours that disrupt economic and social order, as well as activities that harm users' physical health or infringe upon their personal dignity and mental health.

On disclosure obligations, the draft requires service providers to prominently notify users that they are interacting with an AI system rather than a human being. This requirement reflects a broader CAC policy approach to AI transparency that has also appeared in earlier instruments, including the Provisions on the Administration of Deep Synthesis Internet Information Services (2022) and the Interim Measures for the Administration of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services (2023). The stated purpose of the draft, as announced by the CAC, is to promote the sound development and standardised application of AI-powered anthropomorphic interaction services within China's information environment.

The CAC did not specify in the published notice the duration of the public consultation period or the anticipated timeline for finalisation of the measures.

rows.title: China GB/T 45654-2025: National Standard on Basic Safety Requirements for Generative AI Services
rows.summary:
China's Standardisation Administration (SAC), through National Technical Committee 260 on Cybersecurity (SAC/TC260), has issued GB/T 45654-2025, the national recommended standard establishing basic safety requirements for generative artificial intelligence services, applicable across mainland China.

On 4 April 2024, SAC/TC260 published technical documentation titled 'Basic Safety Requirements for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services' (生成式人工智能服务安全基本要求), which CSET has described as the immediate precursor informing the finalised GB/T 45654-2025 national standard. The documentation was drafted by a consortium of institutions including the China Electronics Standardisation Institute (CESI), the National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team/Coordination Centre of China (CNCERT/CC), Zhongguancun Laboratory, Zhejiang University, Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications, and Beijing Baidu Netcom Science and Technology Co., Ltd.

The standard establishes oversight processes that generative AI service providers operating in China must adopt across three principal areas: model training data, model-generated content, and associated safety controls. The documentation names more than 30 specific safety risks. Some, including algorithmic bias, disclosure of personally identifiable information, and copyright infringement, are recognised in international AI safety frameworks. Others are specific to the Chinese regulatory context, including prescriptive guidelines on how generative AI systems must respond to questions concerning China's political system and Chinese history, reflecting the requirements of the tightly regulated domestic internet environment.

A notable addition relative to a preliminary draft released in October 2023 is a clause requiring a supply chain security assessment of the underlying hardware and software of Chinese generative AI models. This provision aligns with broader Chinese regulatory priorities around technology supply chain resilience. The standard operates as a recommended national standard (GB/T designation), meaning it carries strong normative weight in informing compliance expectations under related mandatory regulations, including the Interim Measures for the Administration of Generative Artificial Intelligence Services issued by the Cyberspace Administration of China in July 2023.

rows.title: China Issues Trial Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics
rows.summary:
China's central authorities have promulgated the Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics (Trial), establishing a governance framework for ethical oversight of artificial intelligence research, development and application across the jurisdiction.

On 9 October 2023, China's National Science and Technology Ethics Committee, acting under authority delegated by the State Council, published the Measures for Administration of AI Science and Technology Ethics (Trial) (人工智能科技伦理管理办法(试行)). The instrument entered into force on the date of publication.

The Measures set out tiered ethical review requirements for AI-related scientific and technological activities. Organisations conducting AI research and development are required to implement internal ethical review mechanisms and, where activities fall within categories designated as presenting heightened ethical risk, to submit to a formal ethical review by a recognised ethics review body prior to commencing such activities. The instrument identifies the following areas as subject to enhanced scrutiny: AI systems capable of influencing human cognitive behaviour, AI applications in medical and health contexts, AI systems involving personal biometric data processing, and AI used in education and employment decisions. The Measures further provide that institutions engaging in AI activities must establish a dedicated science and technology ethics committee responsible for internal review, risk assessment and incident reporting.

The instrument assigns supervisory and regulatory responsibilities to a range of authorities. The National Science and Technology Ethics Committee retains overall coordination responsibility, while sector-specific regulators, including those overseeing health, education, finance and transportation, bear responsibility for implementation within their respective domains. Provincial and local science and technology administrative departments are charged with supervision of entities within their geographical jurisdictions. The Measures also introduce reporting obligations: institutions must notify competent authorities of ethical incidents or material deviations from approved AI activity parameters. Non-compliance may result in administrative penalties under the existing Science and Technology Progress Law and related instruments, including suspension of research activities and disqualification from public funding programmes.

rows.title: China's TC 260 Publishes Draft Technical Document on AI Application Ethics and Safety Guidance for Public Comment
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC 260) has released version 1.0 of its draft technical document, "Guidelines for AI Application Ethics and Safety" (人工智能应用伦理安全指引), for public consultation, with feedback sought ahead of a 26 April 2026 deadline.

On 17 April 2026, TC 260 published the draft technical document in accordance with the Committee's procedures for the development of technical documents, as set out in its internal work programme rules. The document has been made available for public comment, with all responses required to reach the TC 260 Secretariat no later than 24:00 on 26 April 2026. The Secretariat is contactable via the designated liaison, Lü Feixiao, at the China Electronics Standardization Institute, Beijing.

TC 260 notes that the document is a technical guidance file rather than a mandatory standard. The draft sets out an ethics and safety framework intended to guide organisations deploying artificial intelligence applications, addressing considerations that TC 260 identifies as relevant to responsible AI use within China's information security standardisation landscape. The accompanying compilation note, published alongside the draft, provides the drafting rationale and scope of the document, though the full substantive provisions of the guidance remain subject to the ongoing consultation process.

TC 260 states that the publication follows its broader programme of AI-related standardisation activity, which has previously included technical specifications on large language model data safety and personal information protection in AI systems. TC 260 does not characterise the document as legally binding, and the guidance is framed as a voluntary reference for entities developing or deploying AI applications. Interested parties are directed to submit written comments to the Secretariat at the China Electronics Standardization Institute, No. 1 Andingmenwai Street, Beijing, postal code 100007, or by email to lvfx@cesi.cn.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: China TC 260 Opens Public Consultation on Draft National Standard for Blockchain System Security Implementation
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC 260) has released a draft national standard titled "Cybersecurity Technology: Blockchain System Security Implementation Guide" for public consultation, with a submission deadline of 8 June 2026.

On 9 April 2026, the Secretariat of the National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization published a notice inviting relevant organisations and experts across China to submit comments on the draft standard. The draft, versioned as of 23 March 2026, has been developed following work by the standard's drafting units. TC 260 states that the consultation period is intended to ensure the quality of the standard prior to finalisation.

The notice identifies Zhang Boran as the designated contact person for the consultation process, with responses required to be submitted to the TC 260 Secretariat no later than 8 June 2026. TC 260 notes that the draft standard and its accompanying compilation explanatory notes, dated 25 March 2026, are both available for review by interested parties. The Secretariat is located at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute, 1 Andingmen East Street, Beijing.

TC 260 states that the draft standard falls within the committee's broader programme of developing cybersecurity technical standards under China's national standardisation framework. TC 260 states that the standard addresses security implementation requirements specific to blockchain systems, though the notice does not specify which underlying legislation or existing standards the draft is intended to support or supplement. No further detail on the substantive technical requirements contained within the draft is disclosed in the consultation notice itself. Parties wishing to provide input are directed to submit written feedback via the TC 260 Secretariat's online platform before the stated deadline.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: TC260 Opens Public Consultation on Security Guidelines for OpenClaw-Class Agent Deployment
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC260) has released a draft cybersecurity practice guide setting out security requirements for the deployment and use of OpenClaw-class intelligent agents, opening the document to public consultation until 15 April 2026.

On 31 March 2026, TC260's Secretariat issued notice Wangan Mi Zi [2026] No. 33, formally inviting public comment on the Cybersecurity Standards Practice Guide: Security Guidelines for the Deployment and Use of OpenClaw-Class Intelligent Agents (Consultation Draft). The Secretariat states that the guide was compiled to address security risks arising from the deployment and use of OpenClaw-class agents and to improve the management capabilities of organisations deploying such systems. The consultation is conducted in accordance with the TC260 Secretariat's document management procedures for cybersecurity standards practice guides.

TC260 identifies the purpose of the draft guide as mitigating risks specific to OpenClaw-class agent technologies, a category of AI agent systems. The Secretariat notes that the notice does not detail the substantive technical or administrative requirements contained within the draft, but the document is directed at relevant units and organisations engaged in the deployment and use of such agents. The draft guide is made available as a downloadable attachment alongside the consultation notice on the TC260 portal.

Stakeholders and relevant organisations wishing to submit comments or recommendations are required to do so by 15 April 2026. Responses are to be directed to the TC260 Secretariat, with the designated contact listed as Wang Hansheng, reachable at 010-64102730 or wanghs@cesi.cn. The Secretariat is based at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute, Andingmen East Street, Beijing.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization

rows.title: China's TC 260 Establishes AI Safety Standards Working Group and International Cybersecurity Standards Study Group
rows.summary:
China's National Technical Committee 260 on Information Security Standardization (TC 260) has established two new subordinate bodies, the AI Safety Standards Working Group (WG9) and the Cybersecurity International Standards Study Group (SG1), following approval at the committee's first director's office meeting of 2026.

On 26 March 2026, TC 260 issued notice 20260327-网安字【2026】6号, formally constituting WG9 and SG1 with effect from 27 March 2026. According to the notice, no financial penalties are associated with this administrative decision.

TC 260 states that WG9 is mandated to survey the current landscape and development trends in AI safety standardisation, develop proposals for an AI safety standards framework, and conduct research and drafting of AI safety standards. The notice designates Zhou Bowen, Director of the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, as chair of WG9, with four deputy chairs drawn from the National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team and Coordination Centre, the China Academy of Information and Communications Technology's AI Research Institute, the Ministry of Public Security's Third Research Institute, and the China Information Security Evaluation Centre.

TC 260 states that SG1 is responsible for tracking developments in international cybersecurity standardisation, conducting comparative analysis between domestic and international cybersecurity standards, and organising research and development of China's proposals for international cybersecurity standards. The notice designates Lin Ning, a researcher at the China Electronics Standardisation Institute (CESI), as chair of SG1, with deputy chairs from CESI's International Cooperation Division and Cybersecurity Research Centre, Huawei Technologies' chief network security standards office, and Beijing University of Technology. The notice confirms that full membership lists for both groups are attached as an annex to the official document.
rows.body_name: National TC 260 on Information Security Standardization
